title: Sociological Explanations of Crime
examTip: In evaluation questions, always pair a theory with a named critic or counter-evidence. Examiners credit: 'Durkheim argued X, but critics say Y because Z.' Learn the key theorist, key concept, and one criticism for each theory.
introTips: Durkheim: crime is normal and even functional — boundary maintenance and social change.
Merton (1938): strain between cultural goals and unequal access to legitimate means → crime.
Becker (1963): crime is a label, not an act — labelling creates deviant careers.
Marxists: law serves ruling-class interests; Left/Right Realists take crime seriously as a real problem.
intro: Why do people commit crime? The obvious answer — because they're bad people who make bad choices — doesn't satisfy sociologists. Crime rates vary systematically by class, gender, ethnicity, and age. They rise and fall with unemployment, inequality, and policing priorities. That kind of pattern doesn't come from individual badness — it comes from social structure. Understanding those structures is what sociological theories of crime are for.

keyPoints.heading: Functionalist Explanation: Durkheim
keyPoints.content: Émile Durkheim made the controversial claim that crime is not just inevitable — it is actually functional, meaning it serves a positive role in society. His argument came from noticing that crime exists in every known society: since it is universal, it must serve some purpose. Two key functions: (a) Boundary maintenance — when a crime is committed and punished, the collective moral reaction (outrage, court proceedings, punishment) reinforces shared values and reminds everyone where the moral boundaries lie. The trial of a murderer doesn't just punish — it strengthens society's collective belief that murder is wrong. (b) Social change — sometimes deviance challenges existing, outdated rules and paves the way for progress. Early suffragettes broke the law; civil rights activists in the USA deliberately violated unjust segregation laws. Yesterday's criminal can become tomorrow's hero. The problem arises at the extremes: too much crime (anomie — normlessness, social breakdown) and social cohesion collapses; too little crime and society becomes rigidly conformist, unable to adapt. So crime at a manageable level is not just inevitable but necessary. Criticisms: doesn't explain why some people commit crime and others don't; ignores the harm crime causes to individuals; doesn't explain which specific acts become criminalised.
writeBullets: Durkheim — crime is normal, universal, and even functional
Boundary maintenance — punishment reinforces shared moral values
Social change — deviance challenges unjust rules; suffragettes, civil rights movement
Anomie — normlessness caused by too much crime; social cohesion breaks down
Too little crime — society too rigid, cannot adapt or change
Criticism — ignores harm to victims; doesn't explain who commits crime or why
keyPoints.memTip: Durkheim: crime is society's way of drawing the line AND of crossing it to move forward.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Durkheim's anomie concept is taken up and developed by Merton — make the connection explicit.

keyPoints.heading: Strain Theory: Robert Merton (1938)
keyPoints.content: Robert Merton adapted Durkheim's concept of anomie to explain crime in American society. American culture promotes a universal goal — financial success (the 'American Dream') — and tells everyone they can achieve it if they work hard enough. But the legitimate means to reach that goal (good education, stable employment, promotion pathways) are not equally available to all. Working-class people and ethnic minorities face structural blockages. The resulting strain between the culturally prescribed goals and the unequal access to legitimate means produces deviance. Merton identified five possible adaptations to this strain: (1) Conformity — accept both the goal (success) and the means (work hard, study); the most common response. (2) Innovation — accept the goal, reject the legitimate means; pursue success through crime (drug dealing, fraud) — Merton's explanation for most criminal behaviour. (3) Ritualism — give up on the goal (abandon the dream of success) but continue following the rules mechanically (the overworked clerk who meets every target but has no ambition). (4) Retreatism — reject both goal and means; drop out of society entirely (chronic poverty, homelessness, severe addiction). (5) Rebellion — reject existing goals and means and substitute new ones; the revolutionary who builds an alternative social order. Criticisms: most crime is 'innovation' — but this ignores non-utilitarian crime (vandalism, joy-riding, gang fights) that doesn't pursue financial success. Also ignores white-collar crime — the wealthy also innovate through fraud.
writeBullets: Merton (1938) — strain between culturally prescribed goals (success) and unequal access to means
American Dream — success promoted universally but legitimate means not equally available
Innovation — accept goal, reject legal means → crime (drug dealing, fraud)
Ritualism — abandon goal, keep following rules mechanically
Retreatism — reject both goal and means; dropout from society
Rebellion — reject existing system, substitute new goals and means (revolutionary)
keyPoints.memTip: CIRR-B — Conformity, Innovation, Ritualism, Retreatism, Rebellion. The five adaptations.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget to name ALL five adaptations — drill these as a set. Innovation is the one that explains most crime.

keyPoints.heading: Interactionism and Labelling Theory: Becker (1963)
keyPoints.content: Howard Becker's 'Outsiders' (1963) is the founding text of labelling theory. Becker argued that crime is not inherent in an act — it depends on whether a label is successfully applied. His famous statement: 'Deviance is not a quality of the act the person commits, but rather a consequence of the application by others of rules and sanctions to an

.' Whether you are labelled a criminal depends not just on what you did but on who you are (class, race, gender all affect the chances of being labelled), and on who has the power to define acts as criminal. Moral entrepreneurs: individuals or groups who campaign to get certain behaviours defined as criminal (e.g., the temperance movement campaigning against alcohol in the USA; drug companies lobbying about drug classification). Primary deviance: initial, minor rule-breaking that has not yet been labelled — the person doesn't see themselves as deviant. Secondary deviance (Edwin Lemert): deviance that follows from being publicly labelled — the label becomes a master status that overrides all other identities. A self-fulfilling prophecy: labelled as a criminal, treated as a criminal by police, teachers, employers → increasingly acts as a criminal → deviant career. Criticisms: ignores victims and the harm caused by crime; implies anyone could be equally labelled (ignores that class and race systematically affect labelling); seems to excuse criminal behaviour.
writeBullets: Becker (1963) 'Outsiders' — crime is a label applied by others, not inherent in the act
Moral entrepreneurs — groups that campaign to criminalise certain behaviours
Primary deviance — initial rule-breaking; not yet labelled; person doesn't see themselves as deviant
Secondary deviance (Lemert) — deviance following from being publicly labelled
Master status — the criminal label overrides all other identities
Self-fulfilling prophecy — labelled → treated as criminal → behaves as criminal → deviant career
keyPoints.memTip: Label first, criminal second — Becker says the label creates the criminal, not the crime.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Labelling theory connects with the interactionist perspective (S1) and with ethnicity and crime statistics (S4).

keyPoints.heading: Marxist Explanation: Law, Power and Capitalism
keyPoints.content: Marxists argue that the law is not neutral — it is made by and for the ruling class (bourgeoisie) to protect their property and power. Laws prioritise: property protection (theft, burglary heavily punished), contract enforcement (protects business), criminalisation of working-class collective action (anti-strike legislation). William Chambliss (1975) — 'The Saints and the Roughnecks': studied two groups of teenage boys in the same town. Both groups committed crimes (driving recklessly, drinking under age, petty theft). The middle-class 'Saints' were protected by their social position — teachers saw them as 'good kids having fun', police let them off, they went on to professional careers. The working-class 'Roughnecks' were labelled as delinquent by teachers and police and most ended up in trouble with the law — despite similar behaviour. Gordon (1976) — capitalism itself encourages crime: competition, greed, and exploitation are fundamental capitalist values — crime is a rational and logical response to living under capitalism. Stuart Hall (1978) — 'Policing the Crisis': a moral panic about Black muggers in 1970s Britain was manufactured by the media, police, and politicians — used to distract public attention from economic crisis and rising unemployment, and to justify a more authoritarian state response to working-class unrest. Criticisms: crime exists in non-capitalist societies too; not all crime benefits the ruling class; Marxism tends to focus on class and overlook gender and ethnicity as independent forces.
writeBullets: Law serves ruling-class interests — protects property, enforces contracts, criminalises WC resistance
Chambliss (1975) 'Saints and Roughnecks' — same behaviour, different outcomes by class
Gordon (1976) — capitalism encourages greed and competition → crime is rational
Stuart Hall (1978) 'Policing the Crisis' — moral panic over Black muggers used to distract from economic crisis
WC crime over-policed; ruling class crime largely ignored or lightly punished
Criticism: crime exists in non-capitalist societies; ignores gender and ethnicity
keyPoints.memTip: Marxist on crime: the law is the ruling class's rulebook — written to protect their stuff and criminalise yours.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Hall's 'Policing the Crisis' is also relevant to moral panics and ethnicity/crime — a flexible study to apply widely.

keyPoints.heading: Right Realism and Left Realism
keyPoints.content: Right Realism — James Q. Wilson and Richard Herrnstein (1985), Charles Murray (1990): crime is best understood as a rational choice — individuals weigh costs and benefits, so deterrence works (increase the certainty and severity of punishment). Murray argues that an underclass has emerged — characterised by welfare dependency, worklessness, absent fathers, and a culture of low aspiration — that produces disproportionate crime. Solutions: tougher policing, longer sentences, zero tolerance (the 'Broken Windows' approach developed by Kelling and Wilson in New York — crack down on minor disorder to prevent escalation to serious crime). Criticisms: ignores structural causes (poverty, inequality); 'underclass' concept stigmatises the poor; zero tolerance associated with race-biased policing. Left Realism — Jock Lea and Jack Young (1984): unlike traditional Marxists who dismiss crime statistics as police bias, Left Realists take crime seriously as a real problem for working-class communities — who suffer most as victims. Three causes of crime (the 'square of crime' — interaction between police, public, offender, and victim): (1) Relative deprivation — feeling deprived compared to others around you (not absolute poverty — you can feel relatively deprived while having enough to eat); (2) Marginalisation — exclusion from mainstream society, no political voice, powerlessness; (3) Subculture — a collective response to deprivation that may include criminal solutions. Solutions: reduce inequality, improve community policing, increase police accountability.
writeBullets: Right Realism (Wilson and Herrnstein 1985; Murray 1990) — rational choice; underclass culture; zero tolerance
Broken Windows (Kelling and Wilson) — crack down on minor disorder to prevent serious crime
Left Realism (Lea and Young 1984) — crime is a real problem for working-class communities
Relative deprivation — feeling deprived compared to others, not just in absolute poverty
Marginalisation — excluded from mainstream; no political voice
Subculture — collective response to deprivation that may include criminal solutions
keyPoints.memTip: Right Realists: get tough. Left Realists: get equal. Both take crime seriously but disagree completely on causes.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Left Realism is frequently confused with Marxism — stress the key difference: Left Realists take working-class victimisation seriously rather than dismissing crime stats as ruling-class ideology.

tables.title: Sociological Explanations of Crime: Summary
rows: Crime is normal and functional — reinforces values and enables change
Ignores harm to victims; doesn't explain who commits crime
Strain between culturally prescribed goals and unequal means → crime
Ignores non-utilitarian crime; ignores white-collar crime
Master status; deviant career; self-fulfilling prophecy
Labels create criminals — secondary deviance follows from being publicly labelled
Ignores victims; ignores structural causes; class/race affect who gets labelled
Law protects ruling class; crime rational response to capitalism; moral panics distract from inequality
Crime exists in non-capitalist societies; ignores gender and ethnicity
Crime is a rational calculation; underclass culture produces crime
Stigmatises the poor; ignores structural inequality; zero tolerance racially biased
Crime caused by relative deprivation, marginalisation, and subculture — real problem for working-class communities
Ignores corporate and white-collar crime; relative deprivation hard to measure

1.question: Merton's strain theory identifies five 'adaptations' to the strain between goals and means. Which adaptation describes a person who commits crime to achieve financial success?
options: Innovation — accepts the cultural goal (success) but rejects the legitimate means
Ritualism — gives up on the goal but continues following the rules
Retreatism — rejects both the goal and the means
Rebellion — substitutes entirely new goals and means
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Innovation is the adaptation Merton uses to explain most crime — drug dealing, fraud, theft — all ways of pursuing the culturally approved goal of success through illegitimate means.
1.wrongFeedback: Think about which adaptation involves pursuing the goal (financial success) but through illegal channels. Ritualism gives up on the goal; retreatism gives up on everything; rebellion replaces the whole system.

3.question: Stuart Hall's 'Policing the Crisis' (1978) argued that the moral panic about Black muggers in the 1970s was used to achieve what?
options: Distract public attention from economic crisis and justify a more authoritarian state
Accurately measure the true rate of street robbery in British cities
Support Left Realist arguments about relative deprivation as a cause of crime
Demonstrate that crime was equally distributed across all social classes
3.correctFeedback: Correct. Hall argued from a Marxist perspective that the panic about 'Black muggers' was manufactured — media, police, and politicians amplified the threat to divert attention from economic problems and justify increased state control over working-class communities.
3.wrongFeedback: Hall was highly critical of the crime statistics — he didn't think they accurately measured anything. He argued the panic served a political purpose. What problem was Britain facing in the 1970s that needed a distraction?

commonMistakes: Confusing Merton's anomie with Durkheim's anomie — Durkheim's refers to normlessness from social breakdown; Merton's refers to the strain between culturally prescribed goals and unequal means.
Forgetting all five of Merton's adaptations — in exam answers, listing them shows analytical depth; students often only name innovation and conformity.
Saying labelling theory means 'if you're labelled you will definitely become a criminal' — the self-fulfilling prophecy is a tendency, not a certainty.
Confusing Left Realism with Marxism — Left Realists accept that crime statistics tell us something real about working-class victimisation; Marxists tend to see statistics as police bias.
Not criticising the theory — evaluation questions require you to name a weakness. Every theory has one.
examTips: For 12-mark 'evaluate' questions, structure your answer: theory → key concept → evidence → criticism → counter-theory. This gives you a clear path through all the marks.
Name the sociologist AND the date — 'Merton (1938)' scores better than just 'Merton'.
Chambliss's 'Saints and Roughnecks' (1975) study is a real-world example that applies to both Marxism and labelling theory — use it in both contexts.
revisionCardBullets: Durkheim — crime is normal and functional: boundary maintenance and social change
Anomie (Durkheim) — normlessness from too much crime; social cohesion breaks down
Merton (1938) strain theory — strain between goals (success) and unequal means → crime
Five adaptations: Conformity, Innovation, Ritualism, Retreatism, Rebellion
Innovation = crime — accept the goal (success), reject legitimate means
Becker (1963) labelling theory — crime is a label applied by others, not inherent in the act
Primary deviance → labelling → master status → secondary deviance → deviant career
Self-fulfilling prophecy — labelled as criminal → treated as criminal → becomes criminal
Chambliss (1975) Saints and Roughnecks — same behaviour, different outcomes by class
Stuart Hall (1978) Policing the Crisis — moral panic over Black muggers; Marxist analysis
Right Realism (Wilson and Herrnstein 1985; Murray 1990) — rational choice, underclass, zero tolerance
Left Realism (Lea and Young 1984) — relative deprivation, marginalisation, subculture
